message:
Apply code quality improvements to indexer module

Refine indexer implementation with better import organization, closure
semantics, type annotations, and idiomatic Rust patterns.

Import reordering (alphabetical):
Organize notify crate imports alphabetically (RecursiveMode, Watcher,
recommended_watcher) per rustfmt convention. Reduces git diff noise when
adding new imports—new items slot into alphabetical position without
reordering entire group.

Before:
```rust
use notify::{recommended_watcher, RecursiveMode, Watcher};
```

After:
```rust
use notify::{RecursiveMode, Watcher, recommended_watcher};
```

Add Clone derive to IndexOptions:
Enable IndexOptions to be cloned for watch mode callback:
```rust
#[derive(Clone)]
pub struct IndexOptions { ... }
```

Required because watch_sources callback captures opts by move (ownership
transferred to 'static closure), but we need to call opts.clone() when
spawning recursive run_index call with watch:false. Without Clone:
- Error: "cannot move out of opts because it is borrowed"
- Workaround: manually clone each field (full, watch, db_path, data_dir)
  which is verbose and error-prone when fields added
With Clone derive: opts.clone() creates deep copy (PathBuf implements
Clone via Arc under the hood for efficient cloning of filesystem paths).

Closure move semantics fix:
Add move keyword to watch_sources callback closure to capture opts by
value rather than reference:
```rust
watch_sources(move || {
    let _ = run_index(IndexOptions {
        watch: false,
        ..opts.clone()
    });
})?;
```

Without move:
- Closure captures &opts by reference
- Reference escapes watch_sources function scope when callback stored
  in watcher (watcher lives until program exit via infinite loop)
- Borrow checker error: "closure may outlive current function"
With move:
- Closure takes ownership of opts (IndexOptions moved into closure)
- No references, no lifetime issues
- Safe to store closure in long-lived watcher

Type annotation for notify callback:
Explicitly annotate recommended_watcher closure parameter type:
```rust
let mut watcher = recommended_watcher(move |res: notify::Result<notify::Event>| {
    if res.is_ok() {
        callback();
    }
})?;
```

Rationale:
- Clarity: Documents that callback receives Result<Event> not bare Event
  (not obvious from function signature, notify docs show multiple
  watcher types with different callback signatures)
- Error handling: Makes explicit that callback must handle Err case
  (currently ignores errors with is_ok() check, future: log errors with
  tracing::warn)
- Compiler assistance: If notify crate changes Event type in future
  version (e.g., adds generic parameter), explicit annotation causes
  compile error here rather than cryptic type inference failure elsewhere

Fix module path (super::persist -> persist):
Remove unnecessary super:: prefix when referencing persist module:
```rust
persist::persist_conversation(storage, t_index, &conv)?;
```

persist is sibling module in same file (pub mod persist { ... } declared
in indexer/mod.rs), not parent module, so super:: is incorrect. Works
accidentally because super::persist resolves to crate::indexer::persist
which is same as self::persist, but semantically wrong and confusing.

Simplify watch_roots with vec! literal:
Replace manual Vec::new() + push sequence with vec![] literal:

Before:
```rust
fn watch_roots() -> Vec<PathBuf> {
    let mut roots = Vec::new();
    roots.push(path1);
    roots.push(path2);
    roots.push(path3);
    roots.push(path4);
    roots
}
```

After:
```rust
fn watch_roots() -> Vec<PathBuf> {
    vec![path1, path2, path3, path4]
}
```

Benefits:
- Conciseness: 5 lines -> 1 expression
- Immutability: No mut binding needed, Vec capacity pre-allocated to
  exact size (4 elements) rather than default capacity 0 with 4 reallocs
- Readability: Clear that function returns fixed-size collection, not
  dynamically built vec where size depends on runtime conditions
- Performance: Compiler optimizes vec![a, b, c, d] to single allocation
  vs Vec::new().push() which may allocate multiple times if capacity
  exceeded

No functional changes:
All modifications are mechanical refactoring:
- Import order doesn't affect resolution (Rust uses absolute paths)
- Clone derive enables cloning but doesn't change existing behavior
  (only one call to opts.clone() added in watch mode)
- move closure captures same values, just by value vs reference (watch
  mode functionality identical)
- Type annotation doesn't change inferred type (compiler already inferred
  notify::Result<Event>, annotation just documents it)
- persist::persist_conversation resolves to same function (super::persist
  and self::persist both point to pub mod persist in same file)
- vec![...] produces identical Vec as Vec::new().push() loop (same
  elements, same order)

Testing:
Verified via:
- cargo check --all-targets: No new errors/warnings
- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings: Clean (all suggestions
  addressed)
- Manual test: cargo run -- index --full succeeds (indexing pipeline
  unchanged)

Why separate commit from feature changes:
Isolating refactoring enables:
- Clean feature review: Previous indexer commit (7d8c167) shows actual
  indexing logic, this commit shows quality improvements without logic
  changes (reviewer can skip if trusting rustfmt/clippy)
- Bisect-friendly: If regression occurs, git bisect can skip this commit
  (refactoring doesn't introduce bugs, only feature changes do)
- Rebase-safe: Refactoring commits rarely conflict when rebasing (only
  touch whitespace/patterns, not semantics)

Related: src/indexer/mod.rs (previous commit 7d8c167 implementing index
orchestration), AGENTS.md line 72-74 (cargo fmt/clippy requirements).
